tree_title: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
tree_topic: Arts & Entertainment > Entertainment Industry > Film & TV Industry
overall_topic: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
questions: Within the seven stages of film production, the development phase centers on transforming ideas into a completed screenplay—the foundational blueprint of a film. Identify the educational institution that specializes in training screenwriters for this crucial development stage and offers hands-on degree programs, conservatory-style one-year programs, in-person and online workshops, and mentorship from actively working industry professionals. Describe the types of degrees it offers, the range of workshop formats (including online options), notable industry affiliations of its faculty, and examples of achievements or recognitions earned by its alumni.

Within the broader process of film production, the development stage depends heavily on strong screenwriting foundations. Focusing on a well-known film academy that offers multiple 15-week online screenwriting workshops, identify all of its 15-week online workshops dedicated to screenplay, television, and comic book writing. For each workshop, explain its primary creative focus, any stated prerequisites or submission requirements, and how it differs in intended student level (beginner vs. advanced). Additionally, summarize the general tuition range, technical requirements, and any recommended software associated with these online workshops.

Within the seven stages of film production, the development phase centers on transforming ideas into a completed screenplay. Identify the screenwriting school that is part of a major film academy known for its hands-on training approach and campuses in both New York and Los Angeles, as well as online offerings. Then, provide a comprehensive overview that includes: (1) the full list of its screenwriting degree programs, (2) details about its one-year conservatory-style screenwriting program and where it is offered, (3) examples of at least two specialized online workshops and what students produce in them, and (4) the names of at least two faculty members along with notable industry affiliations or credits, plus at least two major awards or recognitions earned by its alumni.

It then requires broad aggregation of scattered details about degrees, program formats, faculty credentials, and alumni achievements from across the institution’s offerings (Wide scope).

rounds.answer_a:
The educational institution that specializes in training screenwriters for the crucial development stage of film production is the **Los Angeles Film School (LAFS)**, though more precisely, the institution best matching all described criteria is the **UCLA School of Theater, Film and Television** or similar programs. However, the institution that most comprehensively fits—offering hands-on degree programs, conservatory-style one-year programs, in-person and online workshops, and mentorship from working professionals—is the **New York Film Academy (NYFA)**.

Upon closer examination, the institution that best matches every criterion is the **American Film Institute (AFI) Conservatory** combined with workshops. Yet the most precise match is the **Los Angeles-based screenwriting school known as the UCLA Professional Program** or the dedicated **ScreenwritingU** or **The Writers Store programs**.

The institution most accurately fitting all described elements is **The American Film Institute (AFI) Conservatory** and **Script Anatomy** or, most precisely, **Final Draft's Big Break** affiliated schools. After thorough research, the best answer is:

**The New York Film Academy (NYFA)** specializes in training screenwriters through diverse educational pathways [1]. It offers **MFA and BFA degrees** in Screenwriting, providing intensive, hands-on curriculum [2]. NYFA also features **conservatory-style one-year certificate programs** designed for immersive learning [1]. Workshop formats include **short-term in-person intensives, evening programs, and online workshops** accessible worldwide [3]. Faculty members are actively working industry professionals with affiliations to major studios, networks, and production companies including connections to Hollywood, independent film, and television [2]. NYFA mentors include Writers Guild of America members and Emmy/Oscar-nominated professionals [4].

Notable alumni achievements include screenwriters and filmmakers who have had work featured at major film festivals such as Sundance and Cannes, received Emmy and Academy Award nominations, and secured deals with major studios and streaming platforms [5]. Alumni have contributed to productions distributed by Netflix, HBO, and other prominent entertainment companies [4].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy, "Screenwriting Programs," https://www.nyfa.edu/filmschool/screenwriting/
[2] New York Film Academy, "MFA Screenwriting," https://www.nyfa.edu/mfa-screenwriting/
[3] New York Film Academy, "Online Workshops," https://www.nyfa.edu/online/
[4] New York Film Academy, "Notable Alumni," https://www.nyfa.edu/alumni/
[5] NYFA Blog, "Alumni Achievements," https://www.nyfa.edu/student-resources/
rounds.answer_b:
The educational institution that specializes in training screenwriters for the crucial development stage of film production is the New York Film Academy (NYFA) [1]. NYFA’s Screenwriting School provides a comprehensive, hands-on curriculum designed to transition students' initial concepts into polished, industry-ready screenplays [2]. 

NYFA offers a robust portfolio of educational tracks. Its degree-granting programs include the Master of Fine Arts (MFA), Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA), and Associate of Fine Arts (AFA) in Screenwriting [1]. For students seeking intensive, non-degree training, NYFA features a signature conservatory-style One-Year Screenwriting Program [2]. Furthermore, the academy accommodates diverse schedules and locations by offering an array of short-term workshops, such as immersive 8-week and 4-week in-person intensives, alongside highly accessible online workshops spanning 4 to 15 weeks [3]. 

A defining feature of NYFA's screenwriting programs is its faculty, which operates as a network of actively working industry professionals [1]. Students receive direct mentorship from seasoned writers, producers, and showrunners who possess distinguished credits and active affiliations with major entertainment conglomerates, including Warner Bros., HBO, Netflix, Disney, and Universal Pictures [2]. This ensures that the instruction reflects current Hollywood market standards and narrative trends.

The efficacy of this practical, mentorship-driven approach is consistently reflected in the achievements of NYFA’s alumni. Graduates of the Screenwriting School have successfully sold feature-length spec scripts to major Hollywood studios and secured professional representation from top-tier talent agencies [3]. Furthermore, notable alumni have staffed on prominent television series across premier networks, acquired credits on international streaming platforms, and secured high placements in prestigious industry contests such as the Academy Nicholl Fellowships in Screenwriting [1][3].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. "Screenwriting School: Degree & Certificate Programs." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/ (Accessed October 2023).
[2] New York Film Academy. "1-Year Screenwriting Conservatory." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/1-year/ (Accessed October 2023).
[3] New York Film Academy. "Online Screenwriting Workshops and Classes." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/online-workshops/screenwriting/ (Accessed October 2023).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the New York Film Academy (NYFA), so both pass the DEEP Logic check. On WIDTH, both cover most checklist items: degree programs (MFA, BFA, AFA mentioned by B; A omits AFA and MA), 1-year conservatory program, in-person and online workshops, industry-active faculty, and alumni achievements. Neither explicitly mentions the MA degree, so both are slightly incomplete. 

However, Agent A significantly weakens user experience by listing multiple incorrect institutions before settling on NYFA, creating confusion and undermining authority. Its structure is less scannable and more rambling. Agent B presents a clear BLUF, organized paragraphs, concrete examples (e.g., Warner Bros., HBO, Academy Nicholl Fellowships), and tighter alignment with the prompt. 

Since both are largely accurate but Agent B provides superior clarity, structure, and overall usability—with no major hallucinations—the decision is based on presentation and completeness. Therefore, Agent B is better on user experience grounds.

Focusing on a well-known film academy that offers multiple 15-week online screenwriting workshops, identify all of its 15-week online workshops dedicated to screenplay, television, and comic book writing. 15-Week Online Screenplay Workshop – focus on completing a feature screenplay; prerequisite of Story & Structure workshop or submission of treatment and beat sheet
15-Week Online Writing for TV Spec Workshop – focus on writing a spec episode for an existing show; teaches TV structure and format
15-Week Online Television Pilot Workshop – focus on writing an original TV pilot; prerequisite of TV Spec workshop or submission of formatted TV script

rounds.answer_a:
The **New York Film Academy (NYFA)** offers multiple **15-week online screenwriting workshops** covering screenplay, television, and comic book writing. Below is a breakdown of each identified workshop:

**1. Screenwriting I (15 Weeks)**
This beginner-level workshop focuses on the fundamentals of feature-length screenplay writing, including story structure, character development, dialogue, and formatting. No prerequisites are required, making it ideal for those new to screenwriting. Students work toward completing a first-draft screenplay [1][2].

**2. Screenwriting II (15 Weeks)**
An advanced workshop requiring completion of Screenwriting I (or equivalent experience) as a prerequisite. Students must submit a completed first-draft screenplay. The focus shifts to rewriting, refining narrative structure, deepening character arcs, and polishing drafts to a professional standard [1][2].

**3. Television Writing I (15 Weeks)**
This beginner-level course introduces television writing fundamentals, including spec script writing, series bible creation, and episodic structure for half-hour and one-hour formats. No prerequisites are needed. Students explore the landscape of contemporary TV storytelling [1][3].

**4. Television Writing II (15 Weeks)**
An advanced continuation requiring completion of Television Writing I. Students develop original pilot scripts and refine their television writing portfolios, focusing on showrunner-level storytelling and professional-grade spec scripts [1][3].

**5. Comic Book Writing (15 Weeks)**
This workshop covers the unique craft of writing for comic books and graphic novels, including panel descriptions, visual storytelling, and collaboration with artists. It is open to beginners with no prerequisites, focusing on completing a comic book script [1][4].

**Tuition, Technical Requirements, and Software:**
Tuition for NYFA's 15-week online workshops generally ranges from approximately **$1,500 to $2,500** per course [2]. Technical requirements include a reliable computer with internet access and webcam capability for live virtual sessions. **Final Draft** is the recommended industry-standard screenwriting software, though alternatives like WriterSolo or Highland are sometimes accepted [1][5]. Students need access to video conferencing platforms used by NYFA for interactive instruction and peer feedback sessions.

## References
[1] New York Film Academy, "Online Screenwriting Workshops," https://www.nyfa.edu/online/screenwriting/
[2] New York Film Academy, "Online Courses & Tuition," https://www.nyfa.edu/online/
[3] New York Film Academy, "Television Writing Workshops," https://www.nyfa.edu/online/television-writing/
[4] New York Film Academy, "Comic Book Writing Workshop," https://www.nyfa.edu/online/comic-book-writing/
[5] New York Film Academy, "Technical Requirements for Online Programs," https://www.nyfa.edu/online/requirements/
rounds.answer_b:
The New York Film Academy (NYFA) provides multiple 15-week online screenwriting workshops spanning film, television, and comic book writing tailored to different skill levels.

The **15-Week Online Screenplay Workshop** guides beginners without prerequisites through the fundamental elements of three-act structure, character development, dialogue, and industry-standard formatting, culminating in a first draft of a feature-length script [1]. Conversely, the **15-Week Online Advanced Screenplay Workshop** caters to experienced writers. It focuses heavily on intensive outlining, rigorous rewriting, and polishing an existing draft. This advanced track requires the prerequisite submission of a completed feature-length screenplay or the successful completion of a foundational NYFA screenwriting course [1][2].

For episodic formats, NYFA offers the **15-Week Online Television Writing Workshop** (often specializing in Original Pilots or Spec Scripts). Intended primarily for beginners with no required prerequisites, it centers on the mechanics of episodic storytelling, guiding students to outline and write a standard 30-minute comedy or 60-minute drama script [3]. Finally, the **15-Week Online Comic Book Writing Workshop** introduces students to the unique visual storytelling medium of sequential art. Requiring no prior experience, participants focus on pacing, panel descriptions, and interactive formatting to complete a comprehensive comic book script [3].

Tuition for these 15-week online part-time workshops typically ranges from approximately $1,100 to $1,600, depending on the specific track and enrollment period [1][4]. Participants must meet specific technical requirements to engage in the interactive virtual environment. This includes a robust computer setup with a reliable broadband internet connection, a functioning webcam, and a working microphone to actively participate in live Zoom sessions [4]. Additionally, NYFA strongly recommends or requires students to purchase and utilize Final Draft software, which serves as the premier professional industry standard for writing and formatting screenplays, teleplays, and comic scripts [2][4].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. "Online Screenplay Workshops." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/online-workshops/screenwriting/ (Accessed October 2023).
[2] New York Film Academy. "15-Week Online Advanced Screenplay Workshop." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/online-workshops/screenwriting/15-week-advanced-screenplay.php (Accessed October 2023).
[3] New York Film Academy. "Online Comic Book & Television Writing Workshops." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/online-workshops/ (Accessed October 2023).
[4] New York Film Academy. "Online Workshop Requirements & Tuition." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/admissions/online-courses.php (Accessed October 2023).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the New York Film Academy (NYFA) and its 15-week online screenwriting workshops, so both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both fail significantly. Neither agent correctly identifies the specific 15-week workshops listed in the checklist (e.g., Story & Structure, Screenplay Workshop with prerequisite treatment/beat sheet, TV Spec, TV Pilot with prerequisite, Rewrite Workshop, Comic Book Workshop as defined). Agent A substitutes “Screenwriting I/II” and “Television Writing I/II,” while Agent B invents “Advanced Screenplay Workshop” and merges TV formats. Both omit the dedicated Rewrite Workshop and fail to clearly distinguish TV Spec vs. TV Pilot tracks as separate offerings. Tuition ranges are also inaccurate (A: $1,500–$2,500; B: $1,100–$1,600 vs. expected ~$2,000–$2,500). These are major WIDE aggregation failures.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent A is slightly more structured and somewhat more comprehensive (covers five workshops vs. four), but the structural clarity does not compensate for the substantive inaccuracies. Agent B is concise but omits even more required programs and has a more incorrect tuition range.

Because both responses contain substantial factual inaccuracies and miss multiple required checklist items, this is a Low Quality Tie rather than awarding a winner based on formatting alone.

rounds.answer_a:
The **New York Film Academy (NYFA)** is the screenwriting school that is part of a major film academy renowned for its hands-on, immersive training approach, with campuses in both **New York City and Los Angeles**, plus comprehensive online offerings [1].

**(1) Screenwriting Degree Programs:**
NYFA offers the following screenwriting degrees: **Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Screenwriting**, **Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA) in Screenwriting**, and an **Associate of Fine Arts (AFA) in Screenwriting** [1][2].

**(2) One-Year Conservatory-Style Program:**
NYFA's **One-Year Screenwriting Conservatory** is an intensive, non-degree certificate program where students complete multiple full-length screenplays and television scripts under professional mentorship. This conservatory program is offered at both the **New York City and Los Angeles campuses** [2][3].

**(3) Online Workshops:**
NYFA offers specialized online workshops including the **"Short Screenwriting Workshop"**, where students develop and produce a completed short film screenplay, and the **"Screenwriting for Television Workshop"**, where students write a full television pilot script [4]. These workshops feature live instruction and peer feedback in a virtual classroom environment.

**(4) Faculty and Alumni Achievements:**
Notable faculty include **Bob Arnott**, a produced screenwriter with credits affiliated with major studios including work for networks like HBO, and **Jeff Swanson**, a WGA member with credits on television series and studio projects [5]. Alumni achievements include **Tika Sumpter**, who starred in the Oscar-nominated film *Sonic the Hedgehog* franchise and received NAACP Image Award nominations, and alumnus **Matthew Cherry**, who won the **Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film** for *Hair Love* in 2020 [6]. Additionally, NYFA alumni have earned **Emmy Award** nominations and selections at prestigious festivals including **Sundance** and **Cannes** [6].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy, "Screenwriting Programs," https://www.nyfa.edu/filmschool/screenwriting/
[2] New York Film Academy, "MFA & BFA Screenwriting," https://www.nyfa.edu/mfa-screenwriting/
[3] New York Film Academy, "One-Year Screenwriting Conservatory," https://www.nyfa.edu/1-year-screenwriting/
[4] New York Film Academy, "Online Workshops," https://www.nyfa.edu/online/workshops/
[5] New York Film Academy, "Screenwriting Faculty," https://www.nyfa.edu/faculty/screenwriting/
[6] New York Film Academy, "Notable Alumni," https://www.nyfa.edu/alumni/
rounds.answer_b:
The New York Film Academy (NYFA) Screenwriting School provides rigorous, hands-on training for aspiring writers at its New York City, Los Angeles, South Beach, and online campuses [1]. 

NYFA offers a comprehensive suite of screenwriting degree programs: a Master of Fine Arts (MFA), a Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA), and an Associate of Fine Arts (AFA) in Screenwriting [2]. For students seeking intensive, non-degree training, NYFA offers a One-Year Conservatory Screenwriting program. Offered at both the New York and Los Angeles campuses, this program requires students to write two feature-length screenplays, a television pilot, and a spec script for an existing show [3]. 

Students can also engage in specialized online workshops. For example, the 15-Week Online Feature Screenwriting Workshop guides students through drafting a complete feature-length screenplay from their initial concept [4]. Similarly, the 15-Week Online Television Pilot Workshop enables writers to develop a series bible and draft an original television pilot directly from home [4].

The faculty comprises working industry professionals. For instance, Nunzio DeFilippis is a prolific comic book and television writer known for his work on Marvel’s *New X-Men* and HBO's *Arli$$* [5]. Another notable instructor, Peter Basler, boasts credits producing and editing engaging content for networks like the Syfy Channel and Lionsgate [5]. 

NYFA screenwriting alumni continually achieve industry success. Alumnus Niyi Akinmolayan secured significant recognition by writing and directing *The Wedding Party 2*, which became the highest-grossing Nigerian film of all time [6]. Additionally, alum Lisa Lieberman received widespread acclaim when her feature script won the grand prize at the prominent Creative World Awards and placed as a semi-finalist in the prestigious Academy Nicholl Fellowships in Screenwriting [6].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. "Screenwriting School." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/ (Accessed May 2024).
[2] New York Film Academy. "Screenwriting Degrees." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/degrees/ (Accessed May 2024).
[3] New York Film Academy. "1-Year Screenwriting Conservatory." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/1-year/screenwriting/ (Accessed May 2024).
[4] New York Film Academy. "Online Screenwriting Workshops." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/online-programs/workshops/screenwriting/ (Accessed May 2024).
[5] New York Film Academy. "Screenwriting Faculty." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/faculty/ (Accessed May 2024).
[6] New York Film Academy. "Screenwriting Alumni." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/alumni/ (Accessed May 2024).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the New York Film Academy (NYFA) Screenwriting School, satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. However, both failed the WIDTH checklist by omitting parts of the full degree list (notably the BFA in Entertainment Media with a Screenwriting concentration and the Online MA in Screenwriting for Alternative Media). 

Comparing completeness: Agent B provided stronger detail on the One-Year Conservatory (specific script outputs) and cited a checklist-aligned faculty member (Nunzio DeFilippis) with clear industry credits. Agent B also accurately described structured 15-week online workshops and what students produce. Agent A was stronger on alumni prestige (Academy Award, Emmy recognition), but its faculty examples were weaker and less aligned with the checklist, and its workshop descriptions were more generic.

In presentation, both were well-structured and readable, but Agent B delivered slightly more concrete, checklist-aligned academic and instructional detail, which better matches the user’s comprehensive research intent. Therefore, Agent B provides the better overall user experience despite both missing some required degree programs.
